records.content:
If there is a God, he's a great mathematician! ~ Paul M. Dirac

A curated archive tracing the intellectual path from biological neural systems to neuromorphic computation — spanning foundational neuroscience, synaptic plasticity, computational neuron models, network dynamics, memristive physics, and brain-inspired hardware.

110+ works across 10 research domains, covering 1907–2025.

Foundations

Brain biology, synaptic structure, Hebbian learning, LTP/LTD, astrocytes, and the tripartite synapse — the biological substrate from which every other domain derives.

Neuron Models

Hodgkin–Huxley, integrate-and-fire, AdEx, Izhikevich, and FitzHugh–Nagumo. Mathematical abstractions of neural dynamics, from biophysical fidelity to computational efficiency.

Plasticity

STDP, BCM theory, metaplasticity, and rate- and timing-based learning rules. The mechanisms by which synapses encode, consolidate, and forget.

Cultures & MEA

Neuronal cultures, multi-electrode arrays, burst dynamics, and closed-loop stimulation. Learning observed directly in living networks.

Memristors

Chua’s circuit theory, memristive systems, and SPICE models. The fourth fundamental circuit element as a physical analogue of the synapse.

RRAM Devices

Resistive RAM, oxide switching, filament dynamics, and crossbar arrays. Device physics and materials science behind memristive in-memory computing.

Neuromorphic Hardware

Loihi, TrueNorth, SpiNNaker, Neurogrid, and analog VLSI. Silicon architectures designed to run spiking neural networks at scale and ultra-low power.

Network Dynamics

Neural oscillations, gamma and theta rhythms, synchrony, Hopfield networks, and cortical dynamics. Emergent behaviour from coupled neural populations.

Connectomics

Connectome mapping, graph-theoretic analysis, C. elegans, Drosophila, and the Human Connectome Project. The topological structure of neural circuits.

Cortex Physiology

Place cells, grid cells, mirror neurons, visual and auditory cortex. How specialised cortical circuits instantiate specific computational functions.

Emerging Frontiers

Ferroelectric synapses, organic neuromorphic devices, hybrid SNN architectures, and AER systems. The active boundary of the field.

Connectomics is the systematic reconstruction and analysis of neural wiring diagrams at cellular or subcellular resolution. Using serial electron microscopy, expansion microscopy, or light-sheet fluorescence imaging, researchers reconstruct all neurons and synapses in a tissue volume and represent them as a directed, weighted graph. The resulting connectome is the structural substrate within which all neural computation must occur.

A circuit’s connectivity matrix constrains which computations it can perform and which dynamical states it can express. Connectomes provide structural ground truth against which network models can be tested, and they reveal organisational principles, small-world topology, rich-club structure, hub nodes, that are increasingly used as design targets for neuromorphic architectures. The complete wiring of small organisms enables whole-organism neural simulation: the 302-neuron C. elegans connectome, complete since 1986, remains the canonical benchmark.

Connectomes provide structural constraints for the network models in 

Network Dynamics and inform the circuit-level detail in 

Cortex Physiology. Organisational principles revealed here, modularity, sparse connectivity, hub structure, are referenced directly in the architectural design of 

Neuromorphic Hardware.
A complete electron microscopy volume of the brain of adult Drosophila melanogaster
A connectome and analysis of the adult Drosophila central brain
The organization of the human cerebral cortex estimated by intrinsic functional connectivity
Complex brain networks: graph theoretical analysis of structural and functional systems
The human connectome: a structural description of the human brain
The structure of the nervous system of the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans

Cortical physiology examines how specific circuits in the cerebral cortex implement sensory, motor, and cognitive computations. Rather than studying networks in the abstract, this domain analyses the firing patterns, receptive fields, and circuit motifs of identified cell types in specific regions during behaviour. Methods span in vivo electrophysiology, calcium imaging, and optogenetics.

Cortical circuits are the computational benchmarks against which neuromorphic systems are ultimately measured. Place and grid cells demonstrate that high-dimensional cognitive variables can be encoded in low-dimensional population codes: a direct reference for energy-efficient representational schemes in hardware. Visual cortex provided the biological motivation for convolutional neural networks, and its actual circuit mechanisms reveal precisely where that analogy holds and where it breaks.

Cortical physiology motivates the network models in 

Network Dynamics and the structural wiring in 

Connectomics. The coding principles identified here, sparse codes, population vectors, predictive representations, directly inform algorithm design for 

Neuromorphic Hardware.
Reconstruction and simulation of neocortical microcircuitry

Interneurons of the neocortex: diversity, specificity and function

Microstructure of a spatial map in the entorhinal cortex
Cortical activity during motor execution, motor imagery, action observation and imagined action
Action recognition in the premotor cortex
Coincidence detection in the auditory system: 50 years after Jeffress
The hippocampus as a spatial map — preliminary evidence from unit activity in the freely-moving rat
Receptive fields, binocular interaction and functional architecture in the cat’s visual cortex

Neuronal cultures grown on multi-electrode arrays (MEAs) are a reductionist platform for network-level neural dynamics. Dissociated cortical or hippocampal neurons plated onto extracellular electrode grids allow simultaneous recording from hundreds of sites and targeted electrical stimulation. Over days to weeks in vitro, these cultures self-organise into spontaneously active networks: synchronous burst dynamics in early development, avalanche statistics consistent with criticality at maturation, and stimulus-driven connectivity changes throughout.

MEA cultures are the most direct experimental test bed for biological network plasticity outside of sensory or behavioural context. Closed-loop experiments, stimulation conditioned on the network’s own real-time state that give causal access to plasticity rules that passive recording cannot provide. Cultured networks have also operated as biological computing elements in hybrid bio-electronic systems, making this domain relevant beyond measurement into neuromorphic integration.

MEA recordings provide experimental ground truth for the plasticity rules in 

Plasticity and the activity regimes modelled in 

Network Dynamics. Self-organising cultured network behaviour is a biological reference point for what 

Neuromorphic Hardware seeks to replicate.
Burst and inter-burst duration distributions of spontaneous activity in developing neural networks
Dynamics and effective topology underlying synchronization in networks of cortical neurons

An extremely rich repertoire of bursting patterns during the development of cortical cultures

Learning in networks of cortical neurons
Neuronal avalanches in neocortical circuits
Development, learning and memory in large random networks of cortical neurons: lessons beyond anatomy

A new approach to neural cell culture for long-term studies

The use of neuronal networks on multielectrode arrays as biosensors
Recording action potentials from cultured neurons with extracellular microcircuit electrodes

This section collects papers that cross thematic boundaries or represent fields not yet consolidated enough for a dedicated section. Current topics include ferroelectric synaptic devices, organic electrochemical transistors, hybrid SNN-ANN architectures, advanced AER communication systems, stochastic neuromorphic computing, and in-sensor neuromorphic vision.

The frontier of neuromorphic research appears here before it stabilises into a subfield. Ferroelectric and organic devices offer properties oxide RRAM cannot: nanosecond polarisation switching with CMOS compatibility in the former, soft-matter biocompatibility and ion-mediated plasticity in the latter. Hybrid SNN-ANN architectures acknowledge that spiking and rate-coded computation are each optimal for different tasks, and that deployed systems will blend both.

This section draws on device physics from 

RRAM Devices and 

Memristors, architecture from 

Neuromorphic Hardware, and learning theory from 

Plasticity, pushing each toward new materials, new paradigms, and new application contexts.
Ferroelectric synapses

Self-organized nanowire memristors
Advanced memristive neural systems
Large-scale memristive networks for inference
Organic neuromorphic chip
MOSIX chip
Mahowald & Mead AER

Foundations maps the biological substrate that neuromorphic systems approximate: neuronal and glial cell architecture, the molecular mechanics of chemical synaptic transmission, and activity-dependent mechanisms of synaptic modification.
The synapse: a nanometre-scale junction between neurons is the brain’s elementary unit of memory and computation.

Spike-based computing is, at every level of abstraction, an engineering approximation of biological neural circuits. The fidelity and limits of that approximation cannot be assessed without understanding what is being modelled. The plasticity rules implemented in neuromorphic hardware were first characterised in biological tissue.

Biological ground truth established here motivates the models in 

Neuron Models and the rules in 

Plasticity. Synaptic structure also contextualises the device physics in 

Memristors and 

RRAM Devices.
Spike-timing-dependent plasticity: a Hebbian learning rule

Astrocytes, from brain glue to communication elements: the revolution continues

Glutamate-mediated astrocyte–neuron signalling

LTP and LTD: an embarrassment of riches

Synaptic plasticity: taming the beast

Emotion circuits in the brain

Synaptic plasticity and memory: an evaluation of the hypothesis
Tripartite synapses: glia, the unacknowledged partner

Regulation of synaptic efficacy by coincidence of postsynaptic APs and EPSPs
Synaptic interactions between thalamic inputs to simple cells in cat visual cortex — NMDA & LTP
A synaptic model of memory: long-term potentiation in the hippocampus
Theory for the development of neuron selectivity: orientation specificity and binocular interaction in visual cortex
Long-lasting potentiation of synaptic transmission in the dentate area of the anaesthetized rabbit following stimulation of the perforant path
A quantitative description of membrane current and its application to conduction and excitation in nerve
The Organization of Behavior

The memristor is a two-terminal passive circuit element whose resistance is determined by its charge or current history. Postulated by Leon Chua in 1971 as the fourth fundamental circuit element alongside the resistor, capacitor, and inductor, it was physically realised in 2008 in a titanium dioxide thin-film device at HP Labs. Its defining electrical signature: a pinched hysteresis loop in the current-voltage plane that collapses to a single-valued curve at high frequency. This property is shared by a broad class of devices: collectively termed memristive systems unified by a state-variable formulation coupling voltage, current, and an internal physical variable.

Continuous, analogue conductance tuning in response to voltage pulses makes the memristor a natural physical substrate for synaptic weight storage and update. Weight modification is local and requires no separate processor, enabling in-situ implementation of STDP and other spike-driven learning rules. At the array level, memristors placed at crossbar intersections implement dense passive weight matrices capable of analogue vector-matrix multiplication the core operation of neural inference.

Memristor theory provides the conceptual framework for the device physics in 

RRAM Devices and directly motivates the synaptic implementations in 

Neuromorphic Hardware. The plasticity rules these devices seek to implement are covered in 

Plasticity.
Memristive devices for computing

A versatile memristor model with nonlinear dopant kinetics

Memory effects in complex materials and nanoscale systems

The elusive memristor: properties of basic electrical circuits
Memristor — the missing circuit element
SPICE model of memristor with nonlinear dopant drift

How we found the missing memristor

The elusive memristor: properties of basic electrical circuits

The missing memristor found
Memristive devices and systems
Memristor — the missing circuit element

Network dynamics concerns the collective behaviour of neurons coupled through excitatory and inhibitory synapses. Individual neurons fire spikes, but coupled populations generate oscillations, transient synchrony, attractor states, and chaotic trajectories: phenomena with no single-neuron counterpart. These emergent properties are the necessary bridge between cellular mechanisms and systems-level computation.

Functional brain states, memory retrieval, sensory binding, sleep-wake transitions, pathological seizures, are expressed as network-level dynamical phenomena, not single-neuron properties. Neuromorphic systems that interface with or replicate these functions must produce the same dynamical signatures. Attractor network theory provides one of the few tractable mathematical frameworks directly connecting synaptic connectivity structure to computational function.

Network dynamics emerges from the neuron models in 

Neuron Models and the plasticity rules in 

Plasticity. Oscillatory signatures studied here are directly observable in the MEA recordings in 

Cultures & MEA and connect to the circuit-level physiology in 

Cortex Physiology.
Mechanisms of gamma oscillations
A mechanism for cognitive dynamics: neuronal communication through neuronal coherence
Neuronal oscillations in cortical networks
Theta oscillations in the hippocampus
Stimulus-specific neuronal oscillations in orientation columns of cat visual cortex
Neural networks and physical systems with emergent collective computational abilities

Neuromorphic hardware implements networks of spiking neuron and synapse circuits directly in silicon, with computation emerging from spike propagation through a reconfigurable connectivity fabric rather than sequential instruction execution. The architecture is explicitly modelled on biological neural circuits. The field originates in Carver Mead’s subthreshold analog VLSI work at Caltech in the late 1980s.

Event-driven, sparse spike streams enable energy and latency profiles that von Neumann architectures cannot match: inference at milliwatt budgets, real-time processing for dynamic vision sensors, audio pipelines, and implantable BCI systems. Current platforms span a design space from fully digital (IBM TrueNorth, Intel Loihi 2) to mixed-signal analog (Stanford Neurogrid) to large-scale simulation (Manchester SpiNNaker), each encoding different tradeoffs between biological fidelity, on-chip learning, and scalability.

Neuromorphic hardware implements the neuron models from 

Neuron Models and the plasticity rules from 

Plasticity, using 

RRAM Devices or 

Memristors as synaptic elements. Benchmarking against real cortical circuits draws on 

Cortex Physiology and 

Network Dynamics.
Taking brain-inspired computing to the next level with Intel’s Loihi 2 — extended review
Loihi 2: a neuromorphic processor with on-chip learning and programmable neuron models
Loihi: a neuromorphic manycore processor with on-chip learning

A million spiking-neuron integrated circuit with a scalable communication network and interface

Neurogrid: a mixed-analog-digital multichip system for large-scale neural simulations

Neuromorphic electronic circuits for building autonomous cognitive systems

The SpiNNaker project

Neuromorphic electronic systems

Neuron models are mathematical formalisms for single-neuron electrical dynamics. The spectrum runs from conductance-based models resolving individual ion channel kinetics to two-variable abstractions retaining only threshold and reset. Every position encodes a deliberate trade-off: biological fidelity against computational and analytical cost.

Model choice determines which dynamics survive into simulation, which hardware resources a circuit demands, and which plasticity rules can operate. For neuromorphic designers: a neuron model is a circuit specification. For theorists: it is a dynamical system whose geometry must be fully characterised before network-level inference is valid.

Models here abstract from the biophysics in 

Foundations and constitute the computational units assembled in 

Network Dynamics. Model choice directly constrains what is realisable on 

Neuromorphic Hardware.
Dynamical systems in neuroscience: the geometry of excitability and bursting

Cellular basis of working memory
A model of neuronal bursting using three coupled first order differential equations

An active pulse transmission line simulating nerve axon

First integrate-and-fire model of neuron

Synaptic plasticity covers the mechanisms by which connection strength changes in response to neural activity. Timescales span milliseconds (short-term facilitation and depression) to days (long-term potentiation and depression). Plasticity rules specify the exact conditions under which a synapse is strengthened or weakened: the direction, magnitude, and duration of change as a function of activity history.

Plasticity rules are the brain’s learning algorithms. Implementing them in hardware: STDP in memristive devices in particular is a central problem of neuromorphic engineering: it is the path to on-chip, unsupervised learning without offline training. BCM theory and metaplasticity address what STDP alone cannot: long-run stability and homeostatic control in recurrent networks.

Plasticity rules derive from the biological mechanisms in 

Foundations and are validated against recordings in 

Cultures & MEA. Their hardware implementation motivates the work on 

Memristors and 

RRAM Devices.

Phenomenological models of synaptic plasticity based on spike timing

Spike-timing-dependent plasticity: a Hebbian learning rule

Spike-timing-dependent synaptic modification induced by natural spike trains
Rate, timing, and cooperativity jointly determine cortical synaptic plasticity

Competitive Hebbian learning through spike-timing-dependent synaptic plasticity

Emotion circuits in the brain

Synaptic plasticity and memory: an evaluation of the hypothesis

Synaptic interactions between thalamic inputs to simple cells in cat visual cortex — NMDA & LTP
Synaptic modifications in cultured hippocampal neurons: dependence on spike timing, synaptic strength, and postsynaptic cell type
Regulation of synaptic efficacy by coincidence of postsynaptic APs and EPSPs
Metaplasticity: the plasticity of synaptic plasticity
A synaptic model of memory: long-term potentiation in the hippocampus

Resistive RAM (RRAM) stores data as the resistance state of a metal-insulator-metal stack. Switching between high-resistance and low-resistance states proceeds through the formation and rupture of nanoscale conductive filaments: oxygen vacancies or metal cations driven through a thin oxide layer by electric field, Joule heating, and redox reactions. RRAM is among the most mature memristive technologies, with commercial deployment in multiple forms.

RRAM combines the properties neuromorphic computing requires: nanoscale footprint, CMOS compatibility, analogue conductance tuning, low switching energy, non-volatility. Crossbar arrays perform vector-matrix multiplication in the analogue domain at the site of weight storage, eliminating the von Neumann bottleneck entirely. The principal obstacles are stochastic filament dynamics producing cycle-to-cycle variability, and sneak currents corrupting reads in passive arrays without selector devices.

RRAM is the principal physical realisation of the memristive systems theorised in 

Memristors. Array-level integration connects directly to 

Neuromorphic Hardware, and analogue conductance dynamics are evaluated against the plasticity rules in 

Plasticity.
Roadmap on low power e-beam based lithography

A review of memristive neuromorphic devices and circuits
